texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract: Xe is only produced by cryogenic distillation of air, and its availability is limited by the extremely low abundance. Therefore, Xe recovery after usage is the only way to guarantee sufficient supply and broad application. Herein we demonstrate DD3R zeolite as a benchmark membrane material for CO2/Xe separation. The CO 2 permeance after an optimized membrane synthesis is one order magnitude higher than for conventional membranes and is less susceptible to water vapour. The overall membrane performance is dominated by diffusivity selectivity of CO2 over Xe in DD3R zeolite membranes, whereby rigidity of the zeolite structure plays a key role. For relevant anaesthetic composition ( < 5% CO2) and condition (humid), CO 2 permeance and CO 2 /Xe selectivity stabilized at 2.0  10 � 8 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 and 67, respectively, during long-term operation ( > 320 h). This endows DD3R zeolite membranes great potential for on-stream CO 2 removal from the Xe-based closed-circuit anesthesia system. The large cost reduction of up to 4 orders of magnitude by membrane Xe-recycling ( > 99 + %) allows the use of the precious Xe as anaesthetics gas a viable general option in surgery.

texts.orig: Noble gas capture and purification have proven highly challenging. Xenon (Xe) is a precious and useful noble gas for semiconductor fabrication, lighting, aerospace, medical imaging, and anaesthesia. [1] Xe possesses a number of desirable properties as novel anaesthetic gas such as favourable hemodynamics, lack of metabolism, and low solubility. [2]

texts.orig: Currently, Xe is merely produced as a by-product from cryogenic distillation of air. Because of the low abundance in the Earths atmosphere (0.087 ppmv), intensive capital investment and energy consumption lead to an extremely high Xe price ($30,000-$60,000/m 3 gas,STP ). [3] Besides this, its broad application is significantly limited by the 'supply ceiling'. Annually global production is relatively fixed at approximately 9-12 million liters, if solely used as anaesthetic, this would only cover 800,000 treatments, even Xe availability per patient was severely restricted to 15 L. [4] Therefore, Xe recovery is the only feasible way to guarantee sufficient supply and broad application.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Xe recovery from the exhaled anaesthetic gas mainly involves the removal of CO 2 , N2, and O2 . Liquefying/solidifying was previously demonstrated for Xe recovery at � 139.2 8 C; however, it is energy intensive as cryogenic distillation and the recovery rate is as low as 9%. [3b,4] Membrane separation could make it less energy intensive and economically feasible since it does not involve any phase transformation and allows on-stream processing. Small pore zeolite (e.g., SAPO-34, SSZ-13, DDR) represents ideal membrane material for CO2/Xe separation based on the point view of size exclusion. SAPO-34 zeolite membranes were explored for Kr/Xe [5] and N 2 /Xe [6] separation. However, the SAPO pores would be blocked due to the strong water adsorption from humid feed gas as observed by Noble et al., [7] resulting in an impermeability. To eliminate the pore blockage, just recently, Nairs group developed high-silica CHA (SSZ-13) zeolite membranes on low-cost alumina hollow fibers for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separation. [8] These singlechannel ceramic hollow fibers, suffering from low mechanical strength, may not meet the requirements of industrialization. [9] That being said, the development of zeolite membranes applicable to the practical separation of humid Xecontaining gas mixtures (e.g., exhaled anaesthetic gas) is still a challenge.

texts.orig: Herein, a facile method is demonstrated for the efficient fabrication of DD3R zeolite membranes on four-channel hollow fibers. The superior mechanical strength (fracture load > 24 N) [14] was sufficient for the scale-up fabrication of zeolite membrane modules (124 pieces, 0.5 m 2 ) by an ensemble synthesis strategy in our recent study. [15] The structure directing agent (SDA) contents and synthesis time is well optimized. We also clearly demonstrate the suitability of DD3R zeolite membranes for Xe recovery from humid CO 2 / Xe gas mixtures at the anaesthetics relevant composition and condition. The overall membrane performance is determined by the diffusivity selectivity of CO 2 over Xe through DD3R zeolite and their competitive adsorption, as followed from column breakthrough experiment and molecular dynamic simulation. Notably, the DD3R zeolite membranes are highly robust and exhibit a CO2 permeance that is one order magnitude higher than for conventional membranes, and represents a benchmark membrane for CO 2 /Xe separation.

texts.orig: membranes synthesized with different SDA contents. The typical rhombus DDR zeolite crystals gradually dominate the membrane surface, along with membrane thickness decrease, as the SDA content decreases. The spherical crystals formed at high SDA content (SDA/H2O = 16/4000) are SGT zeolite (Figure S1), where the windows are 6MR only and impermeable to CO2. [10b,e,f,16] It has been proposed that the poor dissolution of SDAs is the main reason for SGT zeolite formation. [17] To address the problem, therefore, a complex aging procedure at elevated temperature is previously applied to achieve a milk-like precursor solution. [10c,d] However, a low reproducibility was demonstrated for the preparation of highselective membranes. [10a,b,e-g] Herein, a clear solution was easily obtained at room temperature for lower SDA content (SDA/H2O = 3/4000), which leads to pure DDR. After further optimization of synthesis time (Figure S2 and S3), well-intergrown DD3R zeolite membranes with a thickness of  4.4 m m were achieved by hydrothermal synthesis of 48 h (Figure 1e,f).

texts.orig: CO2/Xe separation performance. The membrane performance was evaluated by single gas permeation with different kinetic diameter. Even though CO 2 (0.33 nm) is bigger than H2 (0.289 nm) and He (0.255 nm), CO 2 displayed maximum permeance of 1.5  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 due to its preferential adsorption and fast diffusion (Figure 2a). The permeance is higher than earlier reported membranes on tubular (  0.84  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 ) [10c] and disk (1.12  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 ) [10d] supports. Moreover, the hollow fiber geometry is commercially more attractive because of the higher packing density (area/volume) in membrane modules. [18] The ideal selectivity was 9 for CO 2 /He, 5 for CO 2 /H2 , 70 for CO 2 /N 2 , 200 for CO 2 /CH4 , 570 for CO 2 /Xe, 1910 for CO2/SF6 , respectively, all much higher than Knudsen selectivity (Figure S4), confirming the membrane quality. The single gas permeation is even better than that of SAPO-34 zeolite membranes ( P CO2  1.0  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 , S CO2/Xe  500) [5c] because of the more elliptical 8MR windows of DDR zeolite (inset of Figure 2a).

texts.orig: To further demonstrate the membrane quality, the contributions of zeolitic and non-zeolitic pores to gas permeance were quantified based on the SF6 permeance at elevated pressure (Figure S5). Over 99.9% of the CO2 transport proceeds through zeolitic pores (Table S2 and S3), clearly demonstrating a nearly defect-free DD3R zeolite membrane. A slight decrease of pure CO 2 permeance with feed pressure ( < 15%, Figure 2b) is attributed to the monotonic decrease of CO2 diffusivity at higher loading by nonlinear adsorption. [13] The Xe permeation is for the largest part attributed to the non-zeolitic pores (Table S3). The CO2 permeance decreased by 50% once an equimolar mixture was applied, indicating a strong blocking effect of Xe on CO 2 permeation. It further decreased to 0.54  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 even at the same partial pressure as single gas. Moreover, this blocking effect was enhanced by increasing Xe molar fraction (Figure 2c and Figure S6). Eventually, a permeance of 0.24  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 was obtained at a CO 2 molar fraction of 5%. The thermodynamic equilibrium has been used to explain the preferential adsorption of Xe over small molecules (e.g., O 2 , N2, and Kr) in small pore zeolite, involving SAPO-34, [5a,c,6] ion-exchanged SAPO-34, [5d] and CHA (Si/Al = 3). [26] However, its effect on the mass transport behaviour of CO2 molecule through DD3R zeolite membranes is unclear yet. [1,27]

texts.orig: The mass transport behaviour was further investigated via dynamic column breakthrough experiments using a gas mixture with low CO 2 concentration, which always encountered in the closed-circuit anaesthesia system. Both gas molecules broke through the column at the same time (  213 sg � 1 , Figure 3b, Figure S9b) followed by a roll-up of CO2 and steadily increasing Xe apparently replacing adsorbed CO2 . Such competitive adsorption with Xe has not been previously observed in zeolite, [5a,28] metal-organic frameworks, [3a,29] or porous organic cages. [30] The self-diffusivity of CO2 through DD3R zeolite is three orders magnitude higher than for Xe (Figure 3c), which is determined by a MaxwellStefan formulation based on single gas permeation (Table S6). The activation energy of diffusion was 8.8 kJmol � 1 for CO 2 and 12.9 kJmol � 1 for Xe, respectively (Table S7). We envision CO 2 molecules quickly occupy the 8MR windows and the accessible [4 3 5 12 6 1 8 3 ] cages so that a maximum uptake was achieved earlier (225 sg � 1 ) than of Xe (283 sg � 1 ), as evidenced by the transient total uptake profiles by integrating the breakthrough curves (Figure 3d). Interestingly, CO2 uptake decreased by  70% while Xe uptake was always increasing until saturation. In this period, Xe adsorbs in the still open sites and displaces pre-adsorbed CO 2 molecules. This competitive adsorption results in rapid elimination of CO2 from the adsorption sites, explaining the roll-up of breakthrough profile. Finally, the equilibrium adsorption was 0.07 CO 2 cage � 1 and 0.69 Xecage � 1 , resulting in an adsorption selectivity of Xe/CO2 (  4.3), a slightly higher than IAST selectivity prediction (  1.5), which is attributed to structural deformation during Xe uptake. [31]

texts.orig: In order to get more insights to DD3R zeolite membrane separation, molecular dynamic simulation was conducted to visualize the effect of competitive adsorption on CO2 permeation. The simulation system is 2 unit cells wide (Figure 4a), which has been proven adequate on molecular diffusion through zeolite membranes. [32] Since DDR zeolite is not orthogonal in all three directions, the structure we used is a fresh cut to maintain periodicity. texts.orig: The apparent contradicting results between the uptake of Xe in DDR particles and the negligible Xe permeation through the zeolite pores of membrane is attributed to the flexibility of the DDR structure in the individual crystalline particles, in contrast with the crystals in the membrane, whose flexibility is suppressed by their strong intergrowth and anisotropic pore structure. Also, intergrowth barriers cannot be excluded. Simulations with rigid and flexible 8MR zeolites support this, flexibility may increase diffusivity by one to three orders of magnitude. [39]

texts.orig: Potential of Xe recovery at relevant anaesthetic conditions. Membrane separation of CO 2 /Xe has been overlooked in the past decades, only carbon molecular sieve (CMS) and MFI zeolite membranes were explored for CO 2 /Xe mixture separation. [34,35] The performance of our hollow fiber DD3R zeolite membranes transcends current upper bound (Figure 5a). CO 2 permeance is one order magnitude higher than that of CMS, [34] PIM-1, [37] PIM-7, [37] PDMS, [36] and ZIF-8 [38] membranes. The pure CO 2 permeance is slightly higher than SAPO-34 zeolite membranes, which is attributed to the smaller transport resistance of hollow fiber. [40] The high permeation flux would reduce the investment of membranebased Xe recovery units and their physical footprint. Therefore, DD3R zeolite would be a benchmark membrane material for CO 2 /Xe separation.

texts.orig: Although water vapour is typically present in the exhaled anaesthetic gas, its influence on membrane performance was not addressed. [34,35] Here, we investigated CO2 permeation under humid condition to determine the potential of DD3R zeolite membranes for on-stream CO2 removal from the closed-circuit anaesthesia system. For this purpose, the feed gas was saturated with 2.3 kPa water vapour. The pure CO 2 permeance decreased but only by 45% (Figure S12a), far better than high-silica SSZ-13 (66-75% reduction) [8a,20] and SAPO-34 (99% reduction) [7] zeolite membranes. Al-containing zeolite frameworks have a stronger interaction with the polar water molecules, leading to higher water uptake and pore blockage. [20] The CO2 permeance reduction here is even less than the all-silica CHA (88% reduction) [21] and DD3R (68% reduction) [10d,12] zeolite membranes (Table S8). texts.orig: The effects of water vapour on CO 2 /Xe mixture separation are presented in Figure 5b. The selectivity with humid gas is higher than that under dry conditions. Choi et al. [12] reported CO 2 and N2 were respectively drawn towards and slightly away from 8MR windows. The situation in our case is different because of the easy polarization of Xe atoms and its strong interaction with zeolite. The same CO 2 permeance in a dry and humid feed (Figure 5b and Figure S12a) indicates no cage blockage by water adsorption at temperatures above 100 8 C. However, Xe permeance in a humid mixture still reduced by 55% at 100 8 C, 52% at 125 8 C, and 42% at 150 8 C compared to dry condition. Different from the hydrophobic zeolitic DD3R cage walls, the non-zeolitic pores are decorated with hydroxyl groups and will adsorb water molecules much stronger. In this case, the Knudsen diffusion and viscous flow of Xe were reduced due to the partial blockage of the non-zeolitic pores, while having negligible effects on CO2 permeance.

Considering the similar permeance of N 2 and O2 through DD3R zeolite membranes, [10a,b] only N 2 was used in the following long-term stability test ( > 320 h) in a typical mixture of 5% CO 2 , 30% N2 and 65% Xe. texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, we demonstrated the suitability of DD3R zeolite membranes for Xe recovery at the relevant compositions and conditions of exhaled anaesthetic gas. Hollow fiber DD3R zeolite membranes could be easily fabricated at lower SDA content. The CO 2 permeance and permselectivity were 1.5  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 and 570, respectively, where the separation mechanism is dominated by the diffusivity selectivity of CO 2 over Xe. The rigidity of the zeolitic structure is a key to exclude Xe reaching the desired recovery. A membrane surface barrier for CO 2 permeation is induced by the preferential adsorption and strong interaction of Xe with DD3R zeolite surface, visualized by molecular dynamic simulation. In a binary CO 2 /Xe mixture the CO 2 permeance was reduced to 0.76  10 � 7 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 . The hydrophobic DD3R zeolite membranes are less susceptible to water vapour than other zeolite membranes. Water adsorption even improves selectivity by suppressing Xe transport through non-zeolitic pores. For relevant compositions and under conditions of a closed-circuit anesthesia system, CO 2 perme- ance eventually stabilized at 2.0  10 � 8 molm � 2 s � 1 Pa � 1 with a CO2 /Xe selectivity of 67. The high permeation flux, together with long-term stability ( > 320 h) and the nearly negligible Xe loss, would significantly reduce the investment (CAPEX) of membrane-based Xe recovery units, their physical footprint, and operating cost (OPEX), appealing for the on-stream removal of CO 2 from a closed-circuit anaesthesia system.

texts.orig: DD3R zeolite membranes preparation with low SDA content . Hollow fiber DD3R zeolite membranes were prepared by secondary growth method. Prior to the hydrothermal synthesis in the precursor with a molar composition of 3 ADA: 100 SiO 2 : 50 EDA: 4000 H 2 O, four-channel a -Al 2O3 hollow fibers were seeded with Sigma-1 zeolite by dip-coating. The hydrothermal crystallization was carried out at 140 8 C for 48 h. The ADA trapped in zeolite cages was decomposed by ozone at 200 8 C for 80 h.

texts.orig: Gas separation test . The single gas permeation and mixed gas separation performance were evaluated by the Wicke-Kallenbach technique. [35] The composition of permeate was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (Interscience Compact GC) equipped with a ShinCarbon ST 80/100 column (1 m  0.53 mm) and a thermal conductivity (TCD) detector. The feed gas was saturated with water vapor by bubbling through a water container before passing to the membrane.

texts.orig: Molecular dynamic simulations . An uncharged model for DD3R zeolite was used because it does not have any ions inside the cavity and is weakly polar compared with other zeolites. The framework for DD3R zeolite was obtained from the database IZASC (Structure Commission of the International Zeolite Association). [43] Potential models for Xe and CO 2 include a 12-6 Lennard-Jones contribution, electrostatic, harmonic bond and harmonic angle terms. [44] The system set-up is shown in Figure 4a, which results in a driving force perpendicular to the membrane surface. [45] All simulations are carried out under non-equilibrium conditions using the LAMMPS simulation package. [46] The system volume was kept constant and Nose-Hoover style non-Hamiltonian NVT ensemble was used to maintain the temperature at 300 K. [47] Time integration allowed us to monitor the time evolution of the position and velocities of all molecules in the system. Each time step was 1 fs and the simulations run for 1,000,000 steps for equilibration and another 3,000,000 for sampling density and MSD results.

[47] Time integration allowed us to monitor the time evolution of the position and velocities of all molecules in the system. texts.orig: Column breakthrough experimental . The breakthrough set-up for dynamic adsorption performance determination is based on a packed adsorption column with pressure and temperature control. Upon step changes in composition the response outlet composition is analyzed by i) Mass Spectrometer, and ii) Compact Gas Chromatograph. To minimize pressure drop and to prevent potential contamination of the main gas pipelines, a pellet sample of DD3R zeolite was prepared following a two-step procedure. First, 0.5 g powder sample was pressed into a disk under 4 tonm � 2 for 3 min. The disk was then carefully broken up using a pestle and mortar and the fragments were sieved for 500-1000 m mpellets. The temperature was set to 298 K and the pressure at 1 bara (absolute pressure). The inlet flow consisted of 1 mLmin � 1 H2, 5.2 mLmin � 1 N2, 1.3 mLmin � 1 CO2, and 3 mLmin � 1 Xe. N 2 was used to dilute the CO 2 /Xe mixture. The outlet flow was carried by 100 mLmin � 1 He. DD3R pellets were activated before the experiment at 423 K and vacuum for 10 h. Time zero is set with the first detection of the non-adsorbing hydrogen.

The temperature was set to 298 K and the pressure at 1 bara (absolute pressure). The inlet flow consisted of 1 mLmin � 1 H2, 5.2 mLmin � 1 N2, 1.3 mLmin � 1 CO2, and 3 mLmin � 1 Xe. N 2 was used to dilute the CO 2 /Xe mixture. The outlet flow was carried by 100 mLmin � 1 He. DD3R pellets were activated before the experiment at 423 K and vacuum for 10 h. Time zero is set with the first detection of the non-adsorbing hydrogen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Characterization . The membrane morphology was observed by emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6010LA, JEOL). Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of the as-prepared DD3R zeolite particle and membranes were recorded in a Bruker-D8 Advance diffractometer using CoK a radiation ( l = 0.178897 nm) or MiniFlex 600 using CuK a radiation ( l = 0.154056 nm). The 2 q range of 5 8 -50 8 was scanned using a scan rate of 0.05 degs � 1 . N 2 (77 K), CO 2 and Xe (273 K and 298 K) adsorption isotherms were recorded in a TriStar II 3020 (Micromeritics) instrument. The samples were degassed at 350 8 C under N 2 flow for at least 15 hours. The coveragedependent isosteric heat of adsorption was calculated using a virialtype expression.
